title: Anaphylaxis personal action card
category: Medical and first · Aid · Disaster · Health
summary: Medical-heavy survival entry for keeping personal allergy plans, epinephrine location, and emergency contacts together. It is designed for offline emergencies and does not replace professional care.
content: Use this entry to organize keeping personal allergy plans, epinephrine location, and emergency contacts together. Start with scene safety, hand hygiene, calm communication, and written timing. Keep the person comfortable, protect privacy, avoid risky experiments, and prepare a clear handover for a doctor, ambulance, clinic, pharmacist, or trained first-aider. Watch especially for: throat swelling, wheeze, faintness, hives with breathing trouble, repeated vomiting, or known severe allergy. In a long emergency, review the person regularly instead of assuming they are stable.
materials: clean water if available. Context: “Anaphylaxis personal action card”.
soap or sanitizer if available — for «Anaphylaxis personal action card»
gloves or clean barrier if available — for «Anaphylaxis personal action card»
paper and pen. Context: “Anaphylaxis personal action card”.
phone or radio if available — for «Anaphylaxis personal action card»
labels for times and medicines — for «Anaphylaxis personal action card»
steps: Check that the place is safe for the helper and the injured or sick person. Apply it specifically within the “Anaphylaxis personal action card” procedure.
Call emergency help or send a reliable person for help when any red flag is present. Apply it specifically within the “Anaphylaxis personal action card” procedure.
Write the time the problem started, what changed, medicines taken, allergies, and known conditions. Apply it specifically within the “Anaphylaxis personal action card” procedure.
Use clean hands and clean barriers before touching wounds, body fluids, food, medicines, infants, or medical devices. Apply it specifically within the “Anaphylaxis personal action card” procedure.
Keep the person warm or cool as appropriate, still if trauma is possible, and protected from crowds. Apply it specifically within the “Anaphylaxis personal action card” procedure.
Recheck and record changes regularly so deterioration is noticed early. Apply it specifically within the “Anaphylaxis personal action card” procedure.
Prepare a short handover note with name, age, symptoms, times, medicines, allergies, and actions already taken. Apply it specifically within the “Anaphylaxis personal action card” procedure.
warnings: Do not use this as a replacement for training, emergency services, a clinician, or local medical guidance. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Anaphylaxis personal action card”.

Do not guess medicine doses, mix medicines without label checking, share prescriptions, or perform invasive procedures. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Anaphylaxis personal action card”.
Escalate urgently if you see: throat swelling, wheeze, faintness, hives with breathing trouble, repeated vomiting, or known severe allergy.
Keep children, elders, pregnancy, disability, immune weakness, and chronic illness in the high-risk group. Treat this as a specific boundary while managing “Anaphylaxis personal action card”.
common_mistakes: Waiting too long after red flags appear while carrying out “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
Not writing times and medicines while carrying out “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
Touching wounds or fluids without hand hygiene while carrying out “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
Letting crowds block air, privacy, or transport while carrying out “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
Assuming a quiet person is stable without rechecking while carrying out “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
alternatives: Use official first-aid training when available as a fallback for “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
Use local emergency numbers and pharmacists for medicine questions as a fallback for “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
Use paper handover notes when phones are dead as a fallback for “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
Use a trusted translator if language blocks care as a fallback for “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
failure_signs: Red flags appear: throat swelling, wheeze, faintness, hives with breathing trouble, repeated vomiting, or known severe allergy
The person becomes harder to wake, breathe, drink, speak, walk, or recognize during “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
Pain, bleeding, swelling, fever, confusion, or weakness gets worse during “Anaphylaxis personal action card”
when_not_to_use: Do not delay emergency care to complete the checklist when using the “Anaphylaxis personal action card” record
Do not move a trauma patient unless staying is more dangerous when using the “Anaphylaxis personal action card” record
Do not force care on a conscious person except immediate life safety situations under local rules when using the “Anaphylaxis personal action card” record
short_term: Stabilize the scene, reduce immediate harm, record key facts, and connect the person to trained help. This note applies specifically to Anaphylaxis personal action card.
long_term: Keep medical records updated, train the group in certified first aid, maintain supplies, and practice respectful handovers. This note applies specifically to Anaphylaxis personal action card.
if_method_fails: Escalate to emergency services, nearest clinic, pharmacist, radio medical consult, or evacuation route; document the failed step and time. This note applies specifically to Anaphylaxis personal action card.
environment_notes: In apartments, heat, smoke, poor ventilation, crowds, no elevator, and water outages can make medical problems worse. Plan quiet space, clean water, light, and transport. This note applies specifically to Anaphylaxis personal action card.
